Updated April 29, 2026
Ecofit Website Project

Project Status &
Build Plan

A living document tracking strategy, decisions, open questions, and build priorities across three working sessions — April 7, April 24, and April 29, 2026.

Prepared for
Dave Johnson, Phil Rankine, Tim Porth, Will
Sessions
April 7 + April 24 + April 29, 2026
Prepared by
Treetop Growth Strategy
Version
3.0 — Living Document
Project Overview

Where We Stand

Three sessions in. Strategy, StoryBrand, and site architecture are locked. Visual direction decision made April 29 — Verkada-inspired layout selected as the model. Flavor mockups are the immediate next deliverable.

3
Strategy sessions complete
7
Pages defined and scoped
4
New messaging themes confirmed April 29
Next
3–4 visual flavor mockups — homepage only
Page URL Priority Status Blocker
Homepage / Build Now Visual direction confirmed — flavor mockups in progress 3–4 visual mockups to be reviewed by team
Get Started in 90 Days /get-started Build Now Outlined — ready to wireframe Installation timeline + sensor photos from team
For Franchisees /franchisees Build Next Brief written, not yet wireframed Persona session with Dave required
For Corporate Operators /enterprise Build Next Brief written, not yet wireframed Masked case study + Dave persona session
How It Works / Platform /how-it-works Build Next Outlined — needs product photography Sensor hardware photos + dashboard screenshot
About /about Build Next Direction from April 7 session is clear Team bios + stat confirmation from Dave
Manufacturer Partners /partners Blocked Not started Will's language document must be complete first
Visual Direction Confirmed — April 29

The team reviewed the comparable companies and aligned on the Verkada site as the closest model for Ecofit's visual and content approach: outcome-led hero, high-level "how it works" section, clean product visualization, no pricing, no jargon. StoryBrand narrative structure remains the content backbone. Next deliverable: 3–4 visual "flavor" mockups of the homepage with consistent messaging but different look and feel for the team to choose from.

01 — April 7 Session

What we learned — Session One

The founding strategy session with Dave, Phil, and Tim established the brand framework, target buyer, and governing principles for the entire website.

5,000+
Gyms running Ecofit today
2
Core pillars: analytics + maintenance
3
Outcome stages: equipment → floor → members
C-suite
Target buyer altitude — not GMs
Product Architecture

Ecofit sells two things: analytics and maintenance. Analytics is the entry point — 99.9% of customers come for it first. Maintenance is the module that pays off the full investment. Camera and space analytics are an emerging layer that augments the core IoT data with floor-level visibility.

The Buyer and the Website's Job

One job: when a VP or C-suite operator lands on the site, they need to know in under 90 seconds that Ecofit is the intelligence layer their facilities need. Existing customers go straight to the portal. The site is purely for prospective buyers and investors.

The "Living Gym" Concept — Approved Brand Metaphor

Tim introduced the defining metaphor: the Living Gym vs. the dead gym. A living gym floor evolves based on real usage data — it shifts equipment, responds to member behavior, and proves new formats before rolling to franchise locations. A dead gym looks the same it did five years ago.

"One's living, one's dead. And to me, it's like, there are three offerings we have here — maximize your equipment, maximize your floor, maximize your members."

— Tim Porth
The Cardinal Rule — Simplicity

Tim's defining insight from his own company: after hiring someone to write the website, the copy was so technical that even Tim couldn't follow it. He rewrote the homepage himself, simplifying it completely. Website sales doubled. This is the governing principle for every Ecofit page.

"We were so intelligent that people had to be impressed. And when I read it, I was like — I don't even understand what we're saying."

— Tim Porth
Key Decisions Made in Session One — These Are Locked
The website is 100% marketing — no portal documentation, no technical specs
Target buyer is C-suite / VP level, not gym floor managers
"The Living Gym" is the approved brand concept
Three-outcome framework replaces any feature-based structure
5,000+ gyms statistic must appear prominently above the fold
Named partners (EOS, Planet Fitness, Woodway, Matrix, Crew) are non-negotiable credibility anchors
No IoT jargon, no gateway specs, no API descriptions on any customer-facing page
"Talk to an Ecofit Analyst" replaces "Request a Demo" as the primary CTA
02 — April 24 Session

What we learned — Session Two

The April 24 session with Tim, Will, and Dave (joining late) expanded the scope from a homepage-first build to a full multi-persona website. Four buyer types were identified and four new pages were scoped.

The Biggest Insight From Session Two

Almost nobody wants data. They want recommendations. Tim and Will both confirmed it from different angles: most operators are not data analysts. They have a day job. They need Ecofit to do the analysis and tell them what to do. This is Ecofit's key differentiator versus PowerBI, Salesforce, and every generic analytics platform — and it should anchor the messaging on every page.

The 90-Day Page — A Critical Addition

Tim's question was direct: "What happens in 90 days?" Will confirmed the timeline is accurate — roughly a month to install and connect, two more months for data to normalize, and by day 90 a customer has fully actionable intelligence. This page answers the buyer's second-most-important question after the homepage and should be built immediately.

"What happens in 90 days is really important. When do you start seeing real data? This is not an expense. This is an investment."

— Tim Porth
Franchise vs. Corporate — Two Different Buyers

Will surfaced a critical distinction: Planet Fitness-model franchisees are fundamentally different from EOS-model corporate operators. The franchisee's specific emotional problem — corporate won't approve equipment changes without proof — is a distinct use case that requires its own page. "Our data gives you a voice to corporate" is the franchise value prop.

"Instead of going 'I think I have too many treadmills,' corporate now says 'prove it.' Ecofit is how you prove it."

— Will
Make It Tangible — Show the Hardware

Tim made a critical point that is currently missing from every design direction: people need to see the sensor. "When I watched Dave pull it out — somebody sees it, it's tangible, they're like, 'Oh, that's cool. Now I get it.'" The How It Works page cannot be built without product photography of the hardware.

Prove It With Real Numbers

Tim: "We've never given good examples of clubs. A large HVLP club that installed this, saw this kind of return — those things are really important." Will acknowledged the current site has outdated testimonials. Three masked case studies — small, mid, large — are a hard requirement before launch.

"This is not theoretical ROI. You're in 5,000 clubs. This is so far beyond product market fit."

— Bill Colbert
03 — April 29 Session

What we learned — Session Three

The April 29 regroup with Dave, Phil, Tim, and Will produced the clearest directional decisions yet — visual model chosen, four new content themes confirmed, and a white paper opportunity identified around the Matrix consultive sales story.

The Biggest Decision From Session Three

Visual direction is now locked. Verkada (verkada.com) was identified by Will as the best model for how Ecofit should present — it marries outcome with product, gives a high-level "this is how you get there" without technical jargon, and doesn't require a large photography budget. Dave confirmed: the website's one job is short, sweet message → book a demo or download something. Tim confirmed StoryBrand is the right narrative frame as long as the customer is the hero, not Ecofit.

Recovery Equipment — A New Bucket

Dave flagged a critical content gap: Ecofit is not just cardio and strength. Recovery is a rapidly growing category and every major gym chain is trying to figure out what to put on the floor. Ecofit can connect recovery equipment too — and that's a significant competitive edge nobody is talking about on the current site. The three-outcome framework must expand to: Cardio + Strength + Recovery + Floor ROI.

"We are not just doing cardio and strength equipment. We are doing recovery and every single big gym chain in the world right now is trying to understand what to put on the floor for recovery."

— Dave Johnson
Competitive FOMO — The Fear Statement

Will raised a messaging angle that has been missing from every draft: the awareness that your competitors are already doing this. The website needs a fear statement — not panic-inducing, but clear. The operators who are winning are making data-driven decisions. The ones who aren't are ceding ground. This maps to the StoryBrand "stakes" section and should sit above the primary CTA.

"All of their competitors are starting to do this. All the big names are going to eat their cake if they don't get on this and if they don't operate effectively."

— Will
The "Dave on Saturdays" Positioning — White Glove Partnership

The single most powerful differentiator revealed in Session Three: EOS has a full BI division, and PF Corp has a full analytics team — and both come to Dave for their data insights. Dave spends Saturday mornings with Marcel from EOS going through club reports together. This is not a software feature. This is a white-glove data partnership. The anti-PowerBI, anti-Salesforce, anti-Tableau position is: we don't give you another dashboard to manage. We sit down with you and tell you what to do with the data.

"We should be talking about the fact that you have a team of data experts that works through this and helps you understand what your dwell time is and what your numbers should be like."

— Dave Johnson
Matrix / YMCA — White Paper Opportunity

Phil described an active consultive sales success: Matrix rep Eric Vehey is wiring up YMCAs with Ecofit sensors, generating usage data, then using that data to justify and sell equipment refreshes. Cost: $6–7K per facility install. Return: full equipment order. Eric is about to replicate this at a second Y and wants to get the story out to the entire Matrix sales team. Will flagged this as a high-value white paper, OEM partnership proof point, and website case study. Phil to work with Will and Eric to document it with real numbers.

"For less than the cost of one treadmill, you are getting the sale. And you're in there, being data-driven."

— Phil Rankine
Absolute Recomp — The Credibility Story

Ecofit's biggest-ever install is happening now: Absolute Recomp, Frisco TX — approximately 330–450 sensors in a single club, one gateway covering the entire facility. The wireless range story (one gateway, one massive building, no Wi-Fi dependency) is one of the most compelling product proof points Ecofit has. Tight data security agreement limits what can be shared publicly, but the team retains discretion to approach them for joint promotion. This story belongs on the How It Works page and potentially the homepage as a credibility anchor — if they grant permission.

"Some of the biggest gyms in the world don't know who we are. If they did search Ecofit, they didn't get what they wanted from the website. We had to go through two or three channels just to make the connection."

— Phil Rankine
Time to Value — What Will Confirmed
Day 1: Asset tracking, status monitoring, error code notifications (if API enabled)
Week 1+: Initial usage snapshots — early signals on what's being used and what isn't
Month 1+: Hardened data, meaningful monthly reports, overview studies
6+ months: Annual trend data, refresh planning, granular comparative studies

Website copy should lead with "you'll see benefits almost immediately" — not with the wait time for longer-horizon features. The long-term value is real, but you keep customers engaged by delivering fast wins first. Phil's framing: "if you don't have the short-term gain, you're going to lose their interest."

Tim's framing: build this into packages — "monitor your club for the short-term refresh" vs. "set up for long-term optimization" — giving buyers a clear entry point.

03 — StoryBrand Framework

The BrandScript

Every page of the website flows from this. Ecofit is the Guide — not the hero. The C-suite gym operator is the hero. Ecofit helps them win.

1
The Character — The Hero

VP or C-suite decision maker at a multi-location gym chain

Responsible for equipment investments, facility performance, and brand competitiveness across every location. Accountable to a board or franchise network. Makes decisions that cost hundreds of thousands of dollars.

What they want: fitness facilities that attract and retain members — and the intelligence to keep their clubs ahead of the competition without relying on gut feeling or vendor relationships.

2
External Problem

Blind capital decisions

Making six-figure equipment and facility decisions without knowing what is actually being used, what is breaking down, or what members want.

2
Internal Problem

Fear of falling behind

The unsettling sense that competitors are making smarter moves — and that your clubs are slipping without you knowing it.

2
Philosophical Problem

Operators deserve better

A gym operator investing millions in their facilities should not have to rely on a salesperson's opinion. Real data should drive real decisions.

3
The Guide — Empathy

Ecofit understands the problem firsthand

"We know what it means to make a $600K equipment investment without being able to see whether half of it sits unused — and we built Ecofit because gym operators deserve better than that."

3
The Guide — Authority

Ecofit has done this, at scale

5,000+ gyms already running Ecofit today
Manufacturer API partnerships: Matrix, Woodway, Crew
Live deployments: EOS, Planet Fitness, and others
Proven infrastructure built — not a startup with a vision
1
The Plan — Step One

Connect

Integrate Ecofit with your existing equipment, cameras, and facility systems. Your current infrastructure becomes the data source — no rip and replace.

2
The Plan — Step Two

See

Access real-time dashboards showing equipment usage, space utilization, and maintenance health across every location. Your digital fitness intelligence — always on.

3
The Plan — Step Three

Act

Optimize your floor layout, extend equipment life, and scale what works across every location — with confidence that comes from data, not intuition.

Without Ecofit
A dead gym floor that never evolves to match member behavior
Equipment failures members feel before you do
Refresh decisions driven by vendor relationships, not data
Watching a competitor's Living Gym outperform yours
With Ecofit
The Living Gym — a floor that adapts to real member behavior
Maintenance savings that pay off the full investment
Proven models you can scale across every franchise location
Intelligence that replaces vendor opinion with real confidence
04 — Website Structure

Page-by-page build plan

Seven pages total. Each one serves a specific job. Click to expand full guidance, section-by-section content recommendations, and what is needed from the team before that page can be built.

Build Now Homepage ecofit.com/
+

Purpose

Tell the overarching Ecofit story using a StoryBrand narrative. Route the right visitor to the right supporting page. Close the emotionally engaged visitor with a demo CTA. Lead with an outcome-first headline anchored to "The Living Gym."

Recommended Section Order

Hero — "Your gym is alive. Are you listening to it?" No product names, no feature descriptions in the hero.

Stats strip — 5,000+ Gyms | Equipment Connected | Manufacturing Partnerships. Phil: "5,000 gyms can't be wrong."

The Problem — external, internal, and philosophical layers (internal layer is currently missing — must be added)

The Guide — 15 years of infrastructure, named brand logos, proven at scale

Three-outcome framework: Maximize Equipment → Floor → Members

The Stakes — cost of inaction (moved above the primary CTA, not buried at the bottom)

Primary CTA — "Talk to an Ecofit Analyst"

Social proof — testimonials, partner logos, key stats

Persona routing — "I operate a franchise →" / "I run a corporate network →" / "I'm a manufacturer →"

Content to Remove Remove Cut

Any section explaining how the technology works — cameras, gateways, API feeds, IoT devices are not homepage content

What We Need From the Team

Client to provide
Treetop can draft

Select narrative direction: Option A (Classic StoryBrand), Option B (Tension First), or Option C (Insight Model) — three wireframed options are ready for review

Updated testimonials — current site testimonials are outdated, at least one references a brand that filed for bankruptcy

Better product tier names — "Smart Asset Tracking," "Vision AI," and "Analytics" are too generic and will conflict with competitors in search

Build Now Get Started in 90 Days ecofit.com/get-started New
+

Why This Page Exists

Tim's exact question was "What happens in 90 days?" Every buyer who leaves the homepage with interest immediately needs this answer. Will confirmed 90 days is accurate. This page closes the hesitant buyer by making the journey concrete and framing it as an investment, not a cost. Bill's reference: Hapana built a "Go Live in 90 Days" page that addressed operator anxieties directly — sales accelerated. Same approach here.

Recommended Section Order

Hero — "From zero to actionable intelligence in 90 days." Lead with the outcome, not the process.

Investment reframe — "This isn't a line item. It's a return." Anchor with one hard ROI stat above the fold.

Phase 1 (Days 1–30) — Install and Connect. Show the hardware. Show what gets connected. Make it tangible.

Phase 2 (Days 31–60) — Data begins normalizing. First patterns appear. "Your floor is starting to talk."

Phase 3 (Days 61–90) — Fully actionable intelligence. First real decision made. Show an example.

The Start Small story — "Most customers begin with 2–3 clubs. 80% expand after seeing the impact." Lower the barrier.

CTA — "Talk to an Ecofit Analyst" + "See How It Works"

What We Need From the Team

Actual installation timeline for a typical 10-club rollout — how long does each phase take?

Photos of the sensor hardware — Tim said seeing it makes it click. Cannot build this page without product photography.

A real "first decision" example — what is the first thing a customer does differently at day 90? Specific, not abstract.

Phase structure, investment framing copy, and three-phase layout — Treetop can draft this once assets are in hand

Build Next For Franchisees ecofit.com/franchisees New
+

The Persona — Franchise Owner-Operator

They own 5–50 locations under a brand like Planet Fitness, Crunch, or Retro Fitness. Corporate controls the equipment schedule. They cannot deviate without proof. Their specific fear: "I know something is wrong in my clubs but I can't get corporate to act without data." Ecofit gives them that data — and that voice. This is a fundamentally different emotional journey than the corporate operator page.

Draft Headline Direction

"Your data. Your voice. Your case to corporate."

Key Outcomes to Feature

Justify equipment deviations with data — not intuition or franchise-owner instinct

Prove new floor layouts in 2 clubs before rolling to 50 — pilot with confidence

Match member behavior at your specific location vs. the brand average

Give your GM real answers when equipment underperforms — before a member complains

What We Need From the Team

Dedicated personas session with Dave — who are the stakeholders in a franchise buying decision? Who feels this pain most acutely?

A Planet Fitness or Crunch franchisee case study (masked) — the page needs proof, not just a promise

A specific example of a franchisee using Ecofit data to win an equipment argument with corporate — this is the emotional centerpiece of the page

Build Next For Corporate Operators ecofit.com/enterprise New
+

The Persona — Multi-Unit Corporate Buyer

The EOS-type buyer. A VP of Operations or C-suite executive managing dozens to hundreds of owned locations. The CFO signs off. The VP drives it. They want network-level visibility, cross-location benchmarking, and ROI they can defend to a board. Tim's framing: it's going to save you $30 million. Lead with that.

Draft Headline Direction

"One view of every machine. Every location. Every dollar."

Key Outcomes to Feature

Cross-location benchmarking — identify which clubs are underperforming before they become a problem

Eliminate dead zones that drain overhead without retention impact

Data-validated equipment refresh cycles — end reactive, relationship-driven replacement

CFO-ready reporting — the board meeting answer, not a data dump to sift through

What We Need From the Team

EOS or similar case study (masked) — specific dollar figure saved, reduction percentage achieved

Personas session with Dave — what does the CFO objection look like? What does the VP of Operations need to see?

Typical enterprise deal structure — how does pricing scale at 50 clubs vs. 500?

Build Next How It Works / Platform ecofit.com/how-it-works
+

Purpose

Make the product tangible. Tim's critical insight: "When people see the sensor, they get it." Right now Ecofit is invisible on the website. This page shows the hardware, explains the connection in plain language, and demonstrates what comes out the other side — without any jargon.

Recommended Section Order

The sensor — photograph, plain-language explanation, "it mounts in minutes and you'll never touch it again"

How it connects — proprietary protocol, not Wi-Fi dependent, "built and rebuilt over 14 years of real-world deployments"

What gets captured — equipment usage, zone traffic, maintenance health, member flow

Where data goes — the always-on portal and the heat map visual (Phil's floor PDF example is the single most compelling product story from either session)

What you get back — recommendations, not just reports. "We don't give you more data to analyze. We tell you what to do."

The 14-year data reliability story — what makes Ecofit's data trustworthy when competitors have failed

What We Need From the Team — This Page Cannot Launch Without These

Product photography — sensor hardware, gateway, installed on equipment. No photography = no page.

Dashboard screenshot or mockup — the portal visualization, ideally the heat map Phil referenced

Better product tier names (Will to provide) before any copy is finalized

The proprietary protocol reliability story translated into plain English — Tim's framing was right: "high density, no Wi-Fi, built over 14 years"

Build Next About ecofit.com/about
+

Direction From Session One

Lead with infrastructure, not origin story. An investor or skeptical VP who lands here should see scale immediately — installs, connections, partnerships — before reading a single team bio. Team bios are brief and secondary to the proof of scale.

"We have the relationships with the manufacturers for the API feeds. We have the camera relationships. We have the tie-ins to the asset management firms. We're not playing lip service."

— Phil Rankine

What We Need From the Team

Confirmed stat set — installs, connections, partnerships — what can be publicly stated?

Team bios for Dave, Phil, Tim — brief, focused on relevant experience

Headshots or team photography

Blocked Manufacturer Partners ecofit.com/partners
+
Do Not Build Until Will's Language Document Is Complete

This page requires fundamentally different messaging than any operator-facing page — it is about ecosystem embedding, OEM licensing, and data partnership, not about buying an analytics product. Will is actively developing the manufacturer partner language document. Building this page without that foundation will result in full rework. Status: hold until Will's document is delivered.

05 — Design References

Comparable companies

The team reviewed these sites on April 29. Reactions are documented below. Verkada was selected as the visual model. Use the evaluation questions to guide the flavor mockup review session.

Group One — Session One Curated References

Databox
Most Relevant
Analytics SaaS that turns business data into visual dashboards. Same category as Ecofit — selling insight to operators who are not data scientists.
Ecofit Application
They lead with real numbers — dashboards created daily, integrations supported, actual ROI figures. They do not say "trusted by thousands," they show you how many. Maps directly to the "5,000 gyms" stat play.
databox.com ↗
Verkada
✓ Team's Choice
Enterprise physical security and building intelligence — cameras, sensors, access control. Sells to C-suite and VP-level operators. 25,000+ organizations.
Team Reaction — April 29
Will: "I liked how the Verkada layout marries outcome with product — this is where you're going AND this is how you'll get there. High-level, no jargon, answers 'how though?' without going into IT implementation detail." Selected as the primary visual and structural model for Ecofit's homepage.
Why It Works for Ecofit
Same buyer altitude, same hardware-that-produces-intelligence challenge, same enterprise B2B visual register. And it doesn't require expensive photography.
verkada.com ↗
VergeSense
Closest Analogy
IoT sensors and occupancy analytics for corporate offices. Literally does for office space what Ecofit does for gym floors.
Ecofit Application
Watch how they explain sensor hardware without making it the hero — floor plan visualizations and utilization graphs do the selling. They talk about decisions, not devices. Direct template for the camera and space analytics story.
vergesense.com ↗
Stripe
Gold Standard
Payment infrastructure for the internet. Technically complex, but their site makes value feel obvious and inevitable to any business buyer.
Ecofit Application
Enterprise confidence without enterprise stuffiness. Hero ties product to revenue. Big brand logos above the fold. Never mentions APIs in the hero — only outcomes. Study the scroll pacing: each section earns the next.
stripe.com ↗
Proof
Stats as Hero
Conversion optimization SaaS. Leads with "25,000+ online businesses use Proof" — the customer count is literally the first thing you read.
Ecofit Application
Phil's line — "5,000 gyms can't be wrong" — is a direct homepage headline. Proof executes this exact idea: put the scale number in the hero and let it do the heavy lifting.
useproof.com ↗
HubSpot
Multi-Persona Nav
CRM and marketing platform serving multiple buyer types — each with distinct needs and entry points into the same product.
Ecofit Application
Study how they handle three distinct buyer profiles without making any one feel like an afterthought. Their Solutions nav structure is the right model for Ecofit's multi-persona pages.
hubspot.com ↗

Group Two — Team Reactions from April 29

The team reviewed these during the April 29 session. Reactions documented directly from the conversation.

Samsara
Word Salad Warning
IoT-based fleet and operations intelligence for enterprise. Connected assets, operational intelligence, 15,000+ customers.
Team Reaction — April 29
Will: "I was going through that going, okay, but what do you do though? They used the term 'operations' as a bucket catch-word for seemingly everything and I ended up having to dig four pages deep to actually understand what they do." Tim noted Samsara's structural layout is sound — hero → trust bar → features — but the language is the failure. A direct warning for Ecofit: don't let "intelligence" or "optimization" become Ecofit's version of their word salad.
samsara.com ↗
Placer.ai
Liked the Concept
Location intelligence and foot traffic analytics for retail, fitness, and commercial real estate.
Team Reaction — April 29
Will: "I liked the interactive search — the concept of actually seeing a tangible result was nice." Dave: "I found it a bit confusing" and noted the interactive element was broken. Consensus: the concept of showing the data in action is good but should be executed cleanly and reliably. Flag for post-MVP consideration.
placer.ai ↗
Verkada
✓ Selected Model
Enterprise physical security and building intelligence. Sells to the same C-suite operators as Ecofit.
Team Reaction — April 29
Will selected this as the structural model: it answers both "what is the outcome?" and "how does it get there?" without technical depth. Phil noted it also doesn't require a large photography budget. Dave and the group aligned. This is the reference site for the flavor mockups.
verkada.com ↗
Mindbody
Category Context
The dominant fitness management software platform. Ecofit's customers almost certainly use Mindbody — a familiar frame of reference for the buyer.
Ecofit Application
Understanding how Mindbody positions itself helps Ecofit clearly differentiate. Also useful for understanding the category visual language — and where to break from it.
Evaluation Question
Does Ecofit feel visually and tonally distinct from this? It should.
mindbodyonline.com ↗
Daxko
Dave's Favorite Layout
Operations and engagement software for health and fitness organizations. Mid-market.
Team Reaction — April 29
Dave: "I personally liked the landing page for Daxko — how it settled and opened up and got right to the point." Note: Dave was responding to the page's structural efficiency, not endorsing the feature-heavy copy. The takeaway is that Dave values immediate clarity over elaborate narrative buildup.
daxko.com ↗
ABC Fitness
Category Contrast
Fitness management and analytics platform. One of the largest in the space. Sells to the same multi-location gym chains as Ecofit.
Ecofit Application
Same buyer, different product. Will noted ABC gets very technical with acronyms and API references — "that's too technical, that's what you pass off." The buyer has probably seen this site. Ecofit must feel noticeably smarter and cleaner.
abcfitness.com ↗

Design galleries to browse as a team

Saaspo
Filterable by page type. Best for live browsing together — fast, visual, no signup required.
saaspo.com ↗
Webflow Blog
35 current examples with written breakdowns of why each one works. Good for pre-session prep.
webflow.com/blog ↗
SiteBuilderReport
40+ examples with analysis. Strong on explaining the reasoning behind each design choice.
sitebuilderreport.com ↗
06 — Messaging

Core copy themes

These themes must appear consistently across every page, section, and CTA. They are the through-line that makes the site feel coherent. Themes 07 and 08 were added from the April 29 session.

Theme 01

Investment, Not Expense

Never position Ecofit as a cost. Always as a return. "It costs X" vs. "It returns 3X." Tim's framing was clear: this is an investment that pays back.

Theme 02

Tell Me What To Do

Operators don't want raw data. They want recommendations. Ecofit's differentiator vs. PowerBI and Salesforce is: "We do the analysis and tell you what to do." Lead with this on every page.

Theme 03

We Sit Down With You

Dave meets with EOS on Saturday mornings. PF Corp has a full BI division and still comes to Dave. The white-glove partnership — a team of data experts who work through the numbers with you — is Ecofit's anti-Tableau, anti-Salesforce positioning.

Theme 04

5,000 Gyms. Proven, Not Promised.

This number belongs in the hero, not a footnote. Alongside named brand logos it answers the buyer's biggest question before they've read the first paragraph.

Theme 05

Optimization Is Ongoing

Will's framing: "Optimization isn't a one-time project, it's an ongoing operational discipline." The industry is always shifting. Ecofit is how you stay ahead of the curve — not a one-and-done data pull.

Theme 06

No Jargon. Ever.

Translate everything. "A connection that never relies on your gym's Wi-Fi." Not "low-latency proprietary wireless protocol." Validate every page with a non-technical reader before publishing.

Theme 07 — Added April 29

Recovery Is the New Battleground

Every major gym chain is trying to figure out their recovery floor right now — and they don't know what they're doing. Ecofit connects recovery equipment too. This must appear in product category messaging alongside cardio, strength, and floor ROI.

Theme 08 — Added April 29

Your Competitors Are Already Doing This

The fear statement: the gap between data-driven operators and legacy operators is widening. Operators who act now build an intelligence advantage their competitors cannot replicate quickly. Belongs in the Stakes section above the primary CTA.

Theme 09 — Added April 29

Start Small, See It Work

Nobody wires 1,000 clubs on day one. Most start with 2–3 clubs, see the impact within weeks, then expand. Making this explicit on the site — and leading with fast time-to-value — lowers the barrier to entry dramatically.

07 — Client Inputs

What we need from the team

Nothing below can be worked around. Each item is a hard dependency for a specific page. The narrative direction decision has been made — the three immediate items have been updated to reflect the April 29 session.

How to Use This List

Assign a name to each row in your next team session. Every item has a natural owner — Dave has the customer data, Will has the product knowledge, Tim has the ops context. Getting these delivered in sequence is what controls the build timeline.

Deliverable Natural Owner Needed For When Needed
Flavor mockup review — pick the visual direction from 3–4 options Dave + Will Homepage — production cannot start until this is chosen Immediate
Matrix / YMCA white paper — cost, timeline, ROI, Eric Vehey's feedback Phil + Will + Eric OEM Partners page, case studies, consultive sales story Immediate
Absolute Recomp promotion permission — what can Ecofit share publicly? Phil (primary contact) Homepage credibility anchor, How It Works page Week 2
Better product tier names (replace: Smart Asset Tracking, Vision AI, Analytics) Will Homepage, How It Works page Immediate
Sensor hardware photography — mounted, close-up, in context Dave / Tim How It Works, 90 Days page Week 2
Dashboard screenshot or heat map mockup Will / Dave How It Works, Homepage Week 2
3 masked case studies — small / mid / large operator Dave Corporate page, Case Studies, Social proof throughout Week 2
Dedicated personas session — Dave joins, franchise + corporate buyer maps Dave + Treetop Franchisee page, Corporate page — neither can be written without this Week 2
Installation timeline — how long is each of the 3 phases for a typical 10-club rollout? Will / Dave 90 Days page Week 2
Confirmed stat set for About page — what can be publicly stated? Dave About page Week 2
Team bios and headshots — Dave, Phil, Tim Dave About page Week 3
Manufacturer partner language document Will Partners page — blocked until this exists Blocked on Will
General pricing range or investment context Dave Corporate page, 90 Days page When Ready
08 — Governing Principles

The rules that govern every page

These emerged directly from both sessions and reflect the team's own thinking. They serve as the filter for every design and copy decision going forward.

01
Ecofit is the Guide, not the Hero
The C-suite gym operator is the hero of this story. Ecofit is the experienced guide who hands them the tools to win. Every headline, section, and CTA should reinforce this. The moment the website starts celebrating Ecofit's technology for its own sake, it has failed.
02
The 90-second clarity test
Tim shared a defining experience: after investing in professional copywriting, the website was so technically written that even he could not follow it. He simplified the homepage himself, and sales doubled. Every Ecofit page passes this test: a VP of Operations at a mid-size gym chain reads it cold and immediately knows what they would get, why it matters, and what to do next. If a section requires a second read, it gets rewritten.
03
Proven, not promised
Competitors build expensive websites claiming they can do things they have never done. Ecofit has done it. Every page communicates this distinction — 5,000 live installs, named partners, built infrastructure — without saying "we're better than the competition." Show the scale. Let the buyer draw the conclusion.
04
Lead with outcomes, not mechanisms
No IoT jargon. No gateways. No API descriptions. No camera specs. The Living Gym, Maximize Your Floor, actionable insights — these are the words. The mechanisms that produce them are for the sales call, the demo, and the implementation team. They do not belong on the marketing site.
05
Tell me what to do — not just what happened
Added from the April 24 session: operators don't want data. They want recommendations. Tim said it. Will's user research confirmed it. The positioning that separates Ecofit from every generic analytics platform is this: we do the analysis and tell you what to do. Every page should reinforce this, not just the platform page.
06
5,000 gyms is a headline, not a footnote
This number is currently buried or absent. It should be one of the first things a visitor sees. Alongside the named partner logos, it answers the buyer's biggest question — "can I trust these people?" — before they have even read the first paragraph. Lead with the proof.
07
This is an investment, not an expense
Added from the April 24 session: Tim's framing was clear. "It's going to save you $30 million over X days. This is not a cost." The CFO who signs off on this deal needs to walk into a board meeting and call it an investment with a defined return period. The website should make that case before the sales call ever happens.
Ecofit Website Project — Treetop Growth Strategy
Prepared April 2026 · Living Document · Last Updated April 29, 2026
